title: Super balances would drop 25pc in a time of severe stress: APRA
full_text:
The financial regulator has warned that the nation's biggest superannuation funds could dump almost $100 billion of shares and bonds in a worst-case scenario of acute economic stress to meet demand for account withdrawals and switching requests, which could cut average member balances by one-quarter.
The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority, in a review of the sector, has found the rapid growth of the pension industry and the concentration of power among a few large funds had magnified risk. In APRA's hypothetical scenario, the big four banks grappled with the most severe liquidity stress experienced in 50 years caused by a run on their deposits and wholesale funding, triggered by a macroeconomic downturn that depreciated the Aussie dollar and asset prices.
Liquidity stress occurs when a bank is unable to meet its short-term financial obligations, such as sudden deposit withdrawals, without suffering unacceptable losses.
In the hypothetical scenario, the country's six largest pension funds - responsible for $1.4 trillion - also had to deal with "member withdrawals and switching significantly surpassing levels observed" during the pandemic.
It also modelled a big disruption at a third-party service provider, such as MUFG, or an outsourced entity, to amplify the pressure in its inaugural stress test of the financial system.
Although APRA's test found the financial system was ultimately resilient to shocks, and that superannuation funds could help stabilise a strained banking sector with equity funding, the regulator argued the growing power of some super funds had increased vulnerabilities.
"Decisions by a small number of large funds could have outsized and more consequential effects across the system," APRA concluded. "The test found superannuation funds need to uplift their capabilities to test severe stress commensurate with the sector's greater systemic footprint."
This stress test provides the fullest picture yet of the risks facing the country's super. Between major funds and self-managed alternatives, the superannuation sector manages $4.5 trillion and is expected to become the second-largest pension pool globally by 2032, after the United States.
It echoes concerns first highlighted by the Reserve Bank of Australia last April, when it said an "extreme but plausible" shock could leave superannuation funds scrambling to shore up liquidity and respond to margin calls on foreign-currency hedges.
The RBA also said the superannuation sector was so large that it wielded outsized power over the broader financial system, especially in periods of market stress, because of its significant exposure to bank debt and a tendency to "herd" around benchmarks to guarantee member returns.
APRA said the funds hold about 40 per cent of the major banks' short-term debt and a similar share of their equity.
Bank deposits from superannuation funds fell by almost 40 per cent in this scenario, half of which was withdrawn in the first two months of the crisis.
Submissions from both the banks and funds on the speed and quantum of the withdrawals "varied widely", APRA said, "reinforcing the uncertainty of how other participants might behave in stress".
The funds would have to sell $81 billion in equities and $11 billion in bonds to meet member requests. The stress conditions, however, took a major toll on member balances. "Investment losses in the scenario resulted in a significant decline in average member balances over the 12-month period, by around 25 per cent for the median fund," the regulator said.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Superannuation and Investments
